Agent1 Round 1: \boxed{B} No — Ustekinumab is explicitly classified as a "human monoclonal antibody" across several sources, indicating it is not a polyclonal antibody. Polyclonal antibodies involve multiple antigen-specific immune responses from different B cells, whereas monoclonal antibodies originate from a single clone of B cells targeting one specific epitope. Since ustekinumab targets specifically the shared p40 subunit of IL-12 and IL-23, its design aligns with monoclonal rather than polyclonal production. Therefore, option B is correct.
Answer: B

In contrast, the text repeatedly identifies monoclonal antibodies—including those listed in the context of obstructive airway diseases—as key components of immunosuppressive therapy. Therefore, based on standard pharmaceutical classifications and absence of support in the evidence, ustekinumab is not a polyclonal antibody.

Retrieved Evidence:
[1] Ustekinumab -- Indications
Ustekinumab is a human monoclonal antibody typically used to treat moderate to severe plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, moderate to severe Crohn disease, or moderate to severe ulcerative colitis (inflammatory bowel disease). Ustekinumab mediates the body's T-cell response by acting as an antagonist against interleukin-12 (IL12) and interleukin-23 (IL23). The drug is FDA approved for use in moderate to severe plaque psoriasis (Ps), particularly in those patients who are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, aged 6 or older. Patients with active psoriatic arthritis (PsA) may use ustekinumab as monotherapy or in combination with methotrexate.
[2] Psoriatic Arthritis -- Treatment Planning -- Monoclonal antibody (mab) TNFi
These are effective for uveitis and inflammatory bowel disease
[3] Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
Ustekinumab is a medication used to manage and treat a variety of inflammatory conditions, including psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. It is in the monoclonal antibody class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for ustekinumab as a valuable agent in treating inflammatory diseases. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease.
[4] Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage and Administration
Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis treatment are based on an initial intravenous weight-based infusion followed by a subcutaneous maintenance schedule. [22] [23] Initial infusion dosage: Weight < 55 kg: 260 mg (2 vials) Weight 55 to 85 kg : 390 mg (3 vials) Weight > 85 kg: 520 mg (4 vials) Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis recommended adult S/C maintenance dosage: 90 mg subcutaneously eight weeks after initial intravenous administration and every eight weeks after that. Renal dosing (including both hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis) and hepatic dosing are undefined.
[5] Psoriatic Arthritis -- Treatment Planning -- Ustekinumab (IL12/23i)
Human mab to IL-12/23 p40 subunit Dose: 45 mg SC every 3 months after loading (90 mg if weight >100 kg) FDA-approved for psoriatic arthritis, psoriasis, and Crohn’s disease
[6] Ustekinumab -- Contraindications -- Caution Recommended
Latex hypersensitivity (with the pre-filled syringe dose form) Patients aged over 60 Chronic or recurrent infections Latent tuberculosis or those with TB risk Malignancy of a history of malignancy prolonged immunosuppressant therapy Allergen immunotherapy Photochemotherapy (PUVA)
[7] Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
Objectives: Explain the mechanism of action of ustekinumab. Describe the indications for the use of ustekinumab. Review the potential adverse event that can accompany ustekinumab use. Outline strategies utilized by healthcare teams to improve care coordination and communication when ustekinumab is being administered. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.
[8] Ustekinumab -- Review Questions
Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.
[9] Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- History and Physical
Toxicity with these biologics can occur in the prehospital or in-hospital setting. Individuals on bsAbs may present unconscious, apneic, and pulseless. Airway, breathing, and circulation must be immediately assessed, and resuscitation should be performed promptly for individuals on cardiorespiratory arrest. A more detailed evaluation may be pursued once the patient is hemodynamically stable.
[10] Ustekinumab -- Adverse Effects -- Common Reactions
Nasopharyngitis Headache Fatigue Vomiting after induction in inflammatory bowel disease Injection site erythema Bronchitis/upper respiratory tract infection Sinusitis Abdominal pain, fever, influenza, and diarrhea while used for maintenance therapy in inflammatory bowel disease Back pain
[11] Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage Forms and Strengths
Subcutaneous injection: Injection: 45 mg/0.5 mL or 90mg/mL solution in a pre-filled syringe Injection: 45 mg/0.5 mL solution in a single-dose vial Intravenous infusion: Injection: 130 mg/26 mL (5 mg/mL) in a single-dose vial
[12] Ustekinumab -- Contraindications -- Absolute Contraindications
Hypersensitivity to ustekinumab or any of the excipients Active infection Active tuberculosis
[13] Ustekinumab -- Toxicity
There have been reports of the rapid development of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma in those patients treated with ustekinumab. These patients often had risk factors for developing this type of cancer, such as being older (greater than 60) and having undergone phototherapy or other immunosuppressant treatment. Patients should be monitored closely for the development of non-melanoma skin cancers. [37] As with all biological drugs, live vaccines should not be administered while on ustekinumab treatment. [38]
[14] Ustekinumab -- Monitoring
The patients must be monitored clinically for the development of severe infections, including TB, jaundice, malignancy, non-infectious pneumonia, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, or serious skin conditions such as exfoliative dermatitis or erythrodermic psoriasis. Patients additionally should be encouraged to report any concerning symptoms. Routine blood tests, including full blood count, urea, electrolytes, liver function tests, and inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), should be checked approximately every 4 to 6 months. [32] [33] Clinical monitoring for efficacy should be carried out using disease-specific measuring systems for psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. Re-testing for hepatitis and HIV can be considered if clinically indicated. There are experimental data to suggest that monitoring serum drug levels may be beneficial during ustekinumab treatment, but this is not the current standard of care. [34]

The initial dose of golimumab is 200 mg (subcutaneous). At week 2, 100 mg (subcutaneous), followed by 100 mg every 4 weeks

[17] UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.
[18] UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.
[19] UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
Immunosuppressants constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes a diverse array of agents used to attenuate immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. Within this class are inhibitory interleukin compounds, which target interleukin pathways to reduce immune activation, and mycophenolate, a purine synthesis inhibitor that limits lymphocyte proliferation. Antilymphocyte serum, a polyclonal antibody preparation, also belongs to the immunosuppressant group, as do azaserin and basiliximab, both of which interfere with T‑cell activation. Cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus are included among them. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.
[20] UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

Immunosuppressants represent a broad class of agents that dampen immune activity through a variety of mechanisms and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. The group includes inhibitory interleukin agents, antimetabolites such as mycophenolate, mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), azaserin, and koformycin, which interfere with nucleotide synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation. Calcineurin inhibitors, exemplified by cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus, block T‑cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin signaling. The mTOR pathway is targeted by everolimus, providing another route to suppress cell growth and immune responses. Monoclonal antibodies and biologic agents such as basiliximab, daklizumab (daclizumab), muromonab‑CD3, and elipticiny act on specific surface receptors or cytokine pathways to prevent lymphocyte activation. Polyclonal preparations like antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) also contribute to immunosuppression by broadly neutralizing immune cells. Glucocorticoids, including meprednisone and paramethason (paramethasone), exert anti‑inflammation effects by altering gene transcription. Additionally, gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, possesses immunosuppressive properties through inhibition of NF‑κB and other signaling cascades. Together, these agents illustrate the extent of pharmacologic strategies used to control immune responses in clinical practice.

Immunosuppressants constitute a therapeutic class that dampens immune system activity and are employed in transplant rejection prophylaxis, autoimmune disease management, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. Within this class, basiliximab, everolimus and gliotoxin are recognized as specific immunosuppressive agents. Additional agents classified as immunosuppressants include koformycin, the mycophenolic acid derivative known as kyselina mykofenolová, the corticosteroid paramethason, and the calcineurin inhibitor takrolimus. The concept of an inhibitory interleukin is also considered a subordinate element of the immunosuppressant category, reflecting that certain interleukin-targeting drugs function as immune-suppressing therapies. Together, these drugs and mechanisms illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic strategies used to control immune responses in clinical practice.

I agree with the original conclusion that ustekinumab is **not** a polyclonal antibody. Multiple sources explicitly classify ustekinumab as a "human monoclonal antibody" and describe its mechanism involving targeted binding to the p40 subunit of IL-12 and IL-23—a hallmark characteristic of monoclonal rather than polyclonal antibodies—which arises from diverse B-cell populations producing varied antigen-binding fragments.
Therefore:
$\boxed{\text{B}}$
Note: Despite some references listing *antilymphocyte serum* as a polyclonal preparation within immunosuppressive classes — an important contrast point — none equate ustekinumab with such formulations; direct classification consistently confirms its status as monoclonal across reliable sources.

agent1.raw_response_preview: \boxed{B} No — Ustekinumab is explicitly classified as a "human monoclonal antibody" across several sources, indicating it is not a polyclonal antibody.
